text: Approximately 44.7% of athletes have low energy availability, with 44.2% of female athletes and 49.4% of male athletes affected.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Approximately 63.0% of athletes are at risk of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs) based on screening tools.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Athletes with low energy availability have decreased run performance, endurance performance, training response, coordination, concentration, judgment, explosive power, and agility compared to athletes with normal energy availability.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Athletes with low energy availability have an increased likelihood of absence from training due to illness.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Athletes with low energy availability have impaired bone health and a higher risk of bone stress injuries.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Athletes in endurance, aesthetic, or weight-class sports are particularly at risk for low energy availability and REDs.
population: endurance, aesthetic, and weight-class sport athletes

text: Early identification and correction of low energy availability can prevent the development of REDs symptoms, reduce bone stress injury risk, and help athletes optimize training adaptations.
population: male and female athletes across sports

text: Screening tools such as the Low Energy Availability in Females Questionnaire (LEAF-Q) should be employed by coaches to identify athletes at risk for low energy availability.
